question: Use each of the five digits $2, 4, 6, 7$ and $9$ only once to form a three-digit integer and a two-digit integer which will be multiplied together. What is the three-digit integer that results in the greatest product?
response:
A concise way to see why 762 is the three‐digit integer giving the maximum product is to try all possible two‐digit numbers (and the corresponding three‐digit numbers from the remaining digits), checking which product is largest.  One finds:

• If the two‐digit number is 94, the remaining digits are {2, 6, 7}, and the largest three‐digit number from those digits is 762.  
  Then 94 × 762 = 71628.

• Checking all other placements of the digits yields smaller products (for instance, 96 × 742 = 71232, 97 × 642 = 62274, etc.).

Thus the arrangement giving the greatest product is 94 × 762, and the required three‐digit integer is 762.
formatted_prompt: Use each of the five digits $2, 4, 6, 7$ and $9$ only once to form a three-digit integer and a two-digit integer which will be multiplied together. What is the three-digit integer that results in the greatest product?

question: Function  $f: [a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ ,  $0<a<b$  is continuous on  $[a,b]$  and differentiable on  $(a,b)$ . Prove that there exists  $c\in(a,b)$  such that  \[ f'(c)=\frac1{a-c}+\frac1{b-c}+\frac1{a+b}.  \]
response:
A convenient (and by now quite standard) way to see why such a point c exists is to construct an auxiliary function whose derivative “captures” the expression 1/(a−c) + 1/(b−c) + 1/(a+b).  A suitable choice is

φ(x) = f(x) + ln(x − a) + ln(b − x) − x/(a + b),

where x ∈ (a, b).  Let us check two things:

1) Compute φ′(x).  
2) Check the limiting behavior of φ′(x) as x → a⁺ and as x → b⁻.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1) Derivative of φ(x)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

•  The derivative of f(x) is f′(x).  
•  The derivative of ln(x − a) is 1 / (x − a).  
•  The derivative of ln(b − x) is −1 / (b − x).  
•  The derivative of −x/(a + b) is −1/(a + b).

Hence,

φ′(x)  
= f′(x) + 1 / (x − a) − 1 / (b − x) − 1/(a + b).

Rewriting the goal f′(c) = 1/(a−c) + 1/(b−c) + 1/(a+b) in a way that matches signs:

Observe that if x ∈ (a, b), then  
•  x − a > 0, so 1/(x − a) is positive,  
•  b − x > 0, so −1/(b − x) is negative.  

One can check that setting φ′(c) = 0 will lead (after rearranging) to exactly the expression f′(c) = 1/(a−c) + 1/(b−c) + 1/(a+b), once one notes that 1/(c − a) = −1/(a − c).  Concretely:

If  
   0 = φ′(c) 
   = f′(c) + 1/(c − a) − 1/(b − c) − 1/(a + b),

then

   f′(c) = 1/(b − c) − 1/(c − a) + 1/(a + b).

But  1/(c − a) = −1/(a − c).  So

   − 1/(c − a)
   =   1/(a − c).

Hence

   f′(c) 
 =  1/(b − c) + 1/(a − c) + 1/(a + b),

which is the
formatted_prompt: Function  $f: [a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ ,  $0<a<b$  is continuous on  $[a,b]$  and differentiable on  $(a,b)$ . Prove that there exists  $c\in(a,b)$  such that  \[ f'(c)=\frac1{a-c}+\frac1{b-c}+\frac1{a+b}.  \]

question:
For a sequence  $x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_n$  of real numbers, we define its  $\textit{price}$  as \[\max_{1\le i\le n}|x_1+\cdots +x_i|.\] Given  $n$  real numbers, Dave and George want to arrange them into a sequence with a low price. Diligent Dave checks all possible ways and finds the minimum possible price  $D$ . Greedy George, on the other hand, chooses  $x_1$  such that  $|x_1 |$  is as small as possible; among the remaining numbers, he chooses  $x_2$  such that  $|x_1 + x_2 |$  is as small as possible, and so on. Thus, in the  $i$ -th step he chooses  $x_i$  among the remaining numbers so as to minimise the value of  $|x_1 + x_2 + \cdots  x_i |$ . In each step, if several numbers provide the same value, George chooses one at random. Finally he gets a sequence with price  $G$ . 

Find the least possible constant  $c$  such that for every positive integer  $n$ , for every collection of  $n$  real numbers, and for every possible sequence that George might obtain, the resulting values satisfy the inequality  $G\le cD$ .

*Proposed by Georgia*
response:
A convenient way to see why the optimal constant is 2 (and why one cannot do better) is to proceed in two steps:

1) Show that for every choice of real numbers, George’s “greedy” arrangement has price G at most 2D, where D is the minimal possible price.  
2) Exhibit an example (for infinitely many n) on which G can be made arbitrarily close to 2D.

Below is a sketch of the usual argument.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1. Why G ≤ 2D always holds
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Let the multiset of given real numbers be {a₁, a₂, …, aₙ}, and suppose Dave’s optimal arrangement (the one that attains price D) is x₁, x₂, …, xₙ.  Denote its partial sums by  
Sᵢ = x₁ + x₂ + … + xᵢ,  
so that max₍₁ ≤ i ≤ n₎ |Sᵢ| = D.

George’s greedy ordering is (g₁, g₂, …, gₙ).  Write the partial sums of George’s sequence as  
Gᵢ = g₁ + g₂ + … + gᵢ,  
so G = max₍₁ ≤ i ≤ n₎ |Gᵢ|.

To prove G ≤ 2D, one shows inductively that, at each step i, George’s choice of gᵢ can be paired with some xⱼ from Dave’s arrangement so that |Gᵢ| ≤ |Sⱼ| + D.  Intuitively:

• Among the remaining unplaced numbers, George chooses gᵢ so as to keep his new partial sum Gᵢ as small as possible in absolute value.  
• One then shows there is a way to “match” gᵢ with some xⱼ from the optimal arrangement in such a way that |Gᵢ − Sⱼ| is at most D.  (This uses the fact that all partial sums Sᵢ lie within ±D, so whichever number George picks cannot shift him by more than 2D from one of those Sᵢ’s.)

Making this argument precise yields an inductive step guaranteeing |Gᵢ| ≤ 2D for each i, whence G ≤ 2D.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2. Why the factor 2 cannot be improved
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

It remains to see that there are examples where George can end up with a price nearly twice as large as D.  One can construct families of real numbers in which:

• The optimal arrangement (Dave’s) can be made to keep partial sums around ±D.  
• George’s greedy choices cause him at some later step to “veer” away from this narrow band, achieving partial sums close to ±2D.

A simple illustrative kind of construction is to place numbers that cancel each other in a careful order so that Dave can keep partial sums ≤ D, but any local/greedy choice at some step forces George well past ±D.  By repeating or scaling these gadgets, one shows that in the worst case G can be made arbitrarily close to 2D.

Hence the smallest universal constant c with G ≤ cD for all choices of real numbers is c = 2.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Answer
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The desired least possible constant is 2.
formatted_prompt:
For a sequence  $x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_n$  of real numbers, we define its  $\textit{price}$  as \[\max_{1\le i\le n}|x_1+\cdots +x_i|.\] Given  $n$  real numbers, Dave and George want to arrange them into a sequence with a low price. Diligent Dave checks all possible ways and finds the minimum possible price  $D$ . Greedy George, on the other hand, chooses  $x_1$  such that  $|x_1 |$  is as small as possible; among the remaining numbers, he chooses  $x_2$  such that  $|x_1 + x_2 |$  is as small as possible, and so on. Thus, in the  $i$ -th step he chooses  $x_i$  among the remaining numbers so as to minimise the value of  $|x_1 + x_2 + \cdots  x_i |$ . In each step, if several numbers provide the same value, George chooses one at random. Finally he gets a sequence with price  $G$ . 

Find the least possible constant  $c$  such that for every positive integer  $n$ , for every collection of  $n$  real numbers, and for every possible sequence that George might obtain, the resulting values satisfy the inequality  $G\le cD$ .

*Proposed by Georgia*

question: Let  $n\geq 1$  be an integer. Find all rings  $(A,+,\cdot)$  such that all  $x\in A\setminus\{0\}$  satisfy  $x^{2^{n}+1}=1$ .
response:
Solution Explanation:

Let A be a (unital) ring satisfying the hypothesis:

  For every nonzero x ∈ A,  x^(2^n + 1) = 1.

We claim that A must in fact be a finite field of order q whose group of units has order dividing 2^n + 1 (i.e., q - 1 ∣ 2^n + 1).  Below is a step-by-step argument.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1. A has no nonzero nilpotent elements
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Suppose x ∈ A is nilpotent and nonzero.  Then x^k = 0 for some k ≥ 1.  In particular, x^(2^n+1) = 0.  But by hypothesis, for x ≠ 0 we must have x^(2^n+1) = 1, a contradiction (0 ≠ 1).  Hence there are no nonzero nilpotent elements in A.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2. A has no nontrivial idempotents
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

An idempotent e is one satisfying e^2 = e.  Suppose e ∈ A is a nontrivial idempotent, meaning e ≠ 0 and e ≠ 1.  Then

  e^(2^n+1) = e • e^(2^n).

Since e^2 = e, one can check inductively that e^m = e for any m ≥ 1.  Thus e^(2^n+1) = e.  On the other hand, by the problem’s hypothesis, since e ≠ 0 we must have e^(2^n+1) = 1.  Therefore e = 1, contradicting our assumption that e ≠ 1 (and e ≠ 0).  

Hence no nontrivial idempotents exist other than 0 and 1.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3. A must be a field (i.e., has no nontrivial two-sided ideals)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A standard fact in ring theory is that if a ring has no nontrivial idempotents, then it cannot decompose as a direct product of two proper ideals.  In particular, because A has 1, if A had a proper nonzero ideal I, the identity 1 could be replaced by an idempotent “cutting out” that ideal.  Concretely:

• In a ring R₁ × R₂ (a direct product), the elements (1,0) and (0,1) are nontrivial idempotents.  
• Having shown above that A has no such nontrivial idempotents, it follows that A cannot decompose as a direct product of two nonzero rings.

Furthermore, the absence of nonzero nilpotent elements forces A to be a reduced ring.  In fact, one can argue more directly that A must be a division ring (in fact a field, as commutativity of multiplication on the nonzero elements also follows from the condition x^(2^n+1) = 1).  One way to see the “division” property:

• From x^(2^n+1) = 1 (x ≠ 0), we deduce that every nonzero x has a (both-sided) inverse x^(2^n), hence is invertible.  
• Thus A is a division ring.  
• In many elementary treatments (especially over commutative rings), the condition “for all nonzero x, x^m = 1” forces commutativity; but even if one proceeds carefully in the noncommutative case, one can still show it collapses to a (commutative) field once you assume the usual definition of “ring” in contest problems (often meaning commutative with identity).

Consequently, A is a field.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4. A must be finite
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Suppose, for a contradiction, that A were an infinite field.  Consider the polynomial f(X) = X^(2^n+1) - 1 in A[X].  If x ∈ A× (the multiplicative group), then by hypothesis x is a root of X^(2^n+1) - 1.  Because every nonzero element x of A is such a root, that would give infinitely many roots of a nonzero polynomial f(X), which is impossible in a field (a nonzero polynomial of degree d cannot have more than d roots in an integral domain, let alone infinitely many).

Hence A cannot be infinite; it must be a finite field.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5. Characterization of finite fields satisfying x^(2^n+1) = 1 
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

If A is a finite field of order q, then its multiplicative group A× is cyclic of order q - 1.  In a cyclic group of order q - 1, every element x ∈ A× satisfies x^(q-1) = 1.  Our additional requirement x^(2^n+1) = 1 for all x ∈ A× means precisely that

  every element’s order divides 2^n + 1.

But in a cyclic group of order q - 1, the exponent is exactly q - 1.  Therefore the condition that x^(2^n + 1) = 1 for all x ∈ A× is equivalent to:

  (q - 1) ∣ (2^n + 1).

Thus:

  q - 1 divides 2^n + 1.

Equivalently, q = (2^n + 1) / d + 1 for some positive divisor d of (2^n + 1); of course q must also be a prime power (since all finite fields have prime-power cardinalities).  

Putting all of these observations together, we conclude:

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Conclusion
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

All rings A satisfying “for all nonzero x, x^(2^n + 1) = 1” must be finite fields of order q where (q - 1) divides (2^n + 1).  Concretely:

  A  ≅  𝔽_q   with   q - 1  ∣  2^n + 1.

These are precisely the solutions.
formatted_prompt: Let  $n\geq 1$  be an integer. Find all rings  $(A,+,\cdot)$  such that all  $x\in A\setminus\{0\}$  satisfy  $x^{2^{n}+1}=1$ .

question: For $\{1, 2, 3, \ldots, n\}$ and each of its nonempty subsets a unique alternating sum is defined as follows. Arrange the numbers in the subset in decreasing order and then, beginning with the largest, alternately add and subtract succesive numbers. For example, the alternating sum for $\{1, 2, 3, 6,9\}$ is $9-6+3-2+1=5$ and for $\{5\}$ it is simply $5$ . Find the sum of all such alternating sums for $n=7$ .
response:
Solution Explanation:

Let X = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}.  For each nonempty subset S ⊆ X, we form its “alternating sum” by listing the elements of S in decreasing order and then adding and subtracting alternately, beginning with a plus.  We want to find the sum of these alternating sums over all nonempty subsets S of X.

A convenient way to handle this is to track the contribution of each fixed element k ∈ X to the grand total.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Counting how k appears with a plus or minus sign
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Fix an element k ∈ {1, 2, …, 7}.  Consider all subsets S that contain k.  When we list S in decreasing order, the position of k (1st largest, 2nd largest, etc.) determines whether k contributes with a plus or a minus sign in the “alternating sum.”

• Let the elements larger than k be all in the set {k+1, k+2, …, 7}, which has 7 − k elements.  
• Let the elements smaller than k be all in the set {1, 2, …, k−1}, which has k − 1 elements.

For k to be the r-th largest element (where r ≥ 1), we must choose exactly r−1 elements from among those larger than k (out of 7 − k).  Once those r−1 larger elements are chosen, any subset of the k − 1 smaller elements can also be in S.  

Hence, if k is the r-th largest element, the number of choices for S is  
  (number of ways to pick r−1 from the 7−k larger elements) × (number of ways to pick any subset from the k−1 smaller elements)  
  = C(7−k, r−1) × 2^(k−1).

The sign of k in position r is positive if r is odd, and negative if r is even (since we alternate +, −, +, −, … starting with + in the first position).

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Summation for each fixed k
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Define the sign factor s(r) = (−1)^(r−1).  Then the total contribution from k to the grand sum is  
  Contribution(k)  
  = k × ∑ (over r) [s(r) × C(7−k, r−1) × 2^(k−1)],  
where r ranges so that r−1 is at most 7−k, namely r = 1, 2, …, 7−k+1.

Since s(r) = (−1)^(r−1), set j = r−1.  Then j goes from 0 up to 7−k, and the summation inside becomes  
  ∑ (j=0 to 7−k) [ (−1)^j × C(7−k, j ) ].

But by the binomial identity,  
  ∑ (j=0 to m) [ (−1)^j × C(m, j ) ] = (1 − 1)^m = 0  
for any positive integer m.  Here m = 7−k.

Therefore:  
• If 7−k > 0 (i.e., k < 7), then the sum of (−1)^j C(7−k, j) is 0, so Contribution(k) = 0.  
• If k = 7, then 7−k = 0, so the sum (−1)^j C(0, j) = C(0, 0) = 1, and hence  
  Contribution(7) = 7 × 2^(6) = 7 × 64 = 448.

Thus, all elements k < 7 contribute 0 in total, and only k = 7 contributes a net nonzero amount.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3.  Final answer
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Summing the contributions from all k = 1, 2, …, 7 gives  
  0 + 0 + … + 0 + 448 = 448.

Hence the sum of all such alternating sums (over all nonempty subsets of {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}) is 448.
formatted_prompt: For $\{1, 2, 3, \ldots, n\}$ and each of its nonempty subsets a unique alternating sum is defined as follows. Arrange the numbers in the subset in decreasing order and then, beginning with the largest, alternately add and subtract succesive numbers. For example, the alternating sum for $\{1, 2, 3, 6,9\}$ is $9-6+3-2+1=5$ and for $\{5\}$ it is simply $5$ . Find the sum of all such alternating sums for $n=7$ .

question:
Simple substances of elements A and B, when heated to a high temperature, form a compound that is hydrolyzed by water with the release of flammable gas; this gas or its salts can be used in the qualitative analysis of certain inorganic metal ions.
Elements A and C form a hard compound that can scratch quartz.
Elements B and C form gases that are soluble in water causing litmus paper turning red in the solution.
A simple substance of one of the elements can react with water after the removal of the oxide film from its surface.
The combination of all three elements in a certain ratio is a colorless, water-soluble salt X that hydrolyzes in an aqueous solution.
Indicate the sum of all coefficients of the first stage/step of the hydrolysis reaction of substance X.
Answer Choices:
(A) 9
(B) 11
(C) 6
(D) 8
response:
A consistent way to identify the three elements is:

• A = Al (aluminum).  
  – It does not normally react with water unless its oxide layer is removed.  
• B = S (sulfur).  
  – With Al, it forms Al₂S₃, which hydrolyzes to give H₂S (flammable gas used in classic qualitative analysis of metal ions).  
• C = O (oxygen).  
  – With Al, it forms Al₂O₃ (corundum), whose hardness (Mohs 9) exceeds that of quartz (Mohs 7).  
  – With S, it forms SO₂ (a gas that dissolves in water to give an acidic solution).  

The salt X made from Al, S, and O in a certain ratio is Al₂(SO₄)₃ (aluminum sulfate), which does indeed hydrolyze in water.  A standard way to write the “first stage” of its hydrolysis (formation of a basic salt plus some free acid) is:

Al₂(SO₄)₃  +  2 H₂O  →  2 Al(OH)SO₄  +  H₂SO₄

Counting coefficients (including the implicit “1” for a species written without a numeric coefficient) gives:

• Left side: 1 (Al₂(SO₄)₃) + 2 (H₂O) = 3  
• Right side: 2 (Al(OH)SO₄) + 1 (H₂SO₄) = 3  

Total = 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 6.

Hence, the sum of all coefficients in this first‐stage hydrolysis is 6; the correct choice is (C).
formatted_prompt:
Simple substances of elements A and B, when heated to a high temperature, form a compound that is hydrolyzed by water with the release of flammable gas; this gas or its salts can be used in the qualitative analysis of certain inorganic metal ions.
Elements A and C form a hard compound that can scratch quartz.
Elements B and C form gases that are soluble in water causing litmus paper turning red in the solution.
A simple substance of one of the elements can react with water after the removal of the oxide film from its surface.
The combination of all three elements in a certain ratio is a colorless, water-soluble salt X that hydrolyzes in an aqueous solution.
Indicate the sum of all coefficients of the first stage/step of the hydrolysis reaction of substance X.
Answer Choices:
(A) 9
(B) 11
(C) 6
(D) 8

question: How many numbers can you get by multiplying two or more distinct members of the set $\{1,2,3,5,11\}$ together?
response:
A concise way to see why the answer is 15 is to note that 2, 3, 5, and 11 are all distinct primes, and 1 does not affect any product:

• Let T = {2, 3, 5, 11}.  
• Any product of two or more distinct elements from S = {1, 2, 3, 5, 11} is the same as the product of one or more elements from T (because including 1 does not change the product).  
• The number of nonempty subsets of T is 2⁴ − 1 = 15; each subset has a unique product since 2, 3, 5, and 11 are distinct primes.  
• Hence there are 15 distinct products you can form by multiplying two or more distinct elements of S.  

Therefore, the answer is 15.
formatted_prompt: How many numbers can you get by multiplying two or more distinct members of the set $\{1,2,3,5,11\}$ together?

question: Find the number of ordered triples $(a,b,c)$ where $a$ , $b$ , and $c$ are positive integers , $a$ is a factor of $b$ , $a$ is a factor of $c$ , and $a+b+c=100$ .
response:
First, let a, b, c be positive integers with the conditions:

1) a divides b,  
2) a divides c,  
3) a + b + c = 100.

Since a divides b and c, write b = aB and c = aC, where B and C are positive integers. Then

a + b + c = a + aB + aC = a(1 + B + C) = 100.

Let S = 1 + B + C. Then S must be a positive integer and  
a · S = 100.  

That is, a is a positive divisor of 100, and S = 100/a. Also, S ≥ 3 because B, C ≥ 1 implies 1 + B + C ≥ 3. Therefore, for each positive divisor a of 100 that yields S = 100/a ≥ 3, we need to count the pairs (B, C) of positive integers satisfying

B + C = S − 1.

The number of positive integer solutions to B + C = n is n − 1. Hence, for B + C = S − 1, the number of solutions is (S − 1) − 1 = S − 2.

Now list the positive divisors a of 100:

1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 50, 100.

Compute S = 100/a for each and count solutions only when S ≥ 3:

• a = 1 ⇒ S = 100, number of (B, C) = 100 − 2 = 98.  
• a = 2 ⇒ S = 50, number of (B, C) = 50 − 2 = 48.  
• a = 4 ⇒ S = 25, number of (B, C) = 25 − 2 = 23.  
• a = 5 ⇒ S = 20, number of (B, C) = 20 − 2 = 18.  
• a = 10 ⇒ S = 10, number of (B, C) = 10 − 2 = 8.  
• a = 20 ⇒ S = 5, number of (B, C) = 5 − 2 = 3.  
• a = 25 ⇒ S = 4, number of (B, C) = 4 − 2 = 2.  
• a = 50 ⇒ S = 2, which is too small (no solutions).  
• a = 100 ⇒ S = 1, also no solutions.  

Adding the valid counts:

98 + 48 + 23 + 18 + 8 + 3 + 2 = 200.

Therefore, the number of ordered triples (a, b, c) satisfying all conditions is 200.
formatted_prompt: Find the number of ordered triples $(a,b,c)$ where $a$ , $b$ , and $c$ are positive integers , $a$ is a factor of $b$ , $a$ is a factor of $c$ , and $a+b+c=100$ .

question: Rectangle $ABCD_{}^{}$ has sides $\overline {AB}$ of length 4 and $\overline {CB}$ of length 3. Divide $\overline {AB}$ into 168 congruent segments with points $A_{}^{}=P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{168}=B$ , and divide $\overline {CB}$ into 168 congruent segments with points $C_{}^{}=Q_0, Q_1, \ldots, Q_{168}=B$ . For $1_{}^{} \le k \le 167$ , draw the segments $\overline {P_kQ_k}$ . Repeat this construction on the sides $\overline {AD}$ and $\overline {CD}$ , and then draw the diagonal $\overline {AC}$ . Find the sum of the lengths of the 335 parallel segments drawn.
response:
A convenient way to see why all the drawn segments are parallel and to sum their lengths is via coordinates.  Place the rectangle so that  
• A = (0,0),  
• B = (4,0),  
• C = (4,3),  
• D = (0,3).  

1) Segments PₖQₖ on AB and CB.  
   • The side AB is of length 4, so dividing it into 168 equal parts puts  
       Pₖ = (4k/168, 0) = (k/42, 0).  
   • The side CB is of length 3, so dividing it into 168 equal parts (from C down to B) puts  
       Qₖ = (4, 3 - 3k/168).  

   The segment PₖQₖ goes from (k/42, 0) to (4, 3 - 3k/168).  A direct slope‐calculation shows  
   slope(PₖQₖ) = (3 - 3k/168) / (4 - k/42) = 3/4,  
   matching the slope of the diagonal AC, which is 3/4.  Hence PₖQₖ ∥ AC.

   Its length can be computed by distance formula:  
   length(PₖQₖ)² = [4 - (k/42)]² + [3 - 3k/168]².  
   One finds  
   length(PₖQₖ) = 5(168 − k)/168.  

   Summing for k = 1 to 167 gives  
   ∑(k=1 to 167) length(PₖQₖ)  
   = ∑(k=1 to 167) 5(168 − k)/168  
   = (5/168) ∑(k=1 to 167) (168 − k).  
   But  
   ∑(k=1 to 167) (168 − k) = 168·167 − (1 + 2 + … + 167) = 168·167 − 167·168/2 = 168·167/2 = 84·167.  
   Hence  
   ∑(k=1 to 167) (168 − k) = 14028,  
   and so  
   ∑(k=1 to 167) length(PₖQₖ) = (5/168)(14028) = 5 × 83.5 = 417.5.  

2) Segments RₖSₖ on AD and CD.  
   • The side AD is of length 3, so points on AD are  
       Rₖ = (0, 3k/168) = (0, k/56).  
   • The side CD is of length 4, so points on CD (from C to D) are  
       Sₖ = (4 − 4k/168, 3) = (4 − k/42, 3).  

   A similar distance calculation shows each RₖSₖ also has length  
   length(RₖSₖ) = 5(168 − k)/168,  
   and hence the sum over k=1 to 167 is again 417.5.

3) The diagonal AC.  
   Clearly, AC = √(4² + 3²) = 5.

Altogether, there are 167 + 167 = 334 parallel segments from steps (1) and (2), plus the diagonal AC, making 335 segments in total. Their total length is  
417.5 + 417.5 + 5 = 840.  

Answer: 840.
formatted_prompt: Rectangle $ABCD_{}^{}$ has sides $\overline {AB}$ of length 4 and $\overline {CB}$ of length 3. Divide $\overline {AB}$ into 168 congruent segments with points $A_{}^{}=P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{168}=B$ , and divide $\overline {CB}$ into 168 congruent segments with points $C_{}^{}=Q_0, Q_1, \ldots, Q_{168}=B$ . For $1_{}^{} \le k \le 167$ , draw the segments $\overline {P_kQ_k}$ . Repeat this construction on the sides $\overline {AD}$ and $\overline {CD}$ , and then draw the diagonal $\overline {AC}$ . Find the sum of the lengths of the 335 parallel segments drawn.

question: Serge and Lis each have a machine that prints a digit from 1 to 6. Serge's machine prints the digits $1,2,3,4,5,6$ with probability $p_{1}, p_{2}, p_{3}, p_{4}, p_{5}, p_{6}$, respectively. Lis's machine prints the digits $1,2,3,4,5,6$ with probability $q_{1}, q_{2}, q_{3}, q_{4}, q_{5}, q_{6}$, respectively. Each of the machines prints one digit. Let $S(i)$ be the probability that the sum of the two digits printed is $i$. If $S(2)=S(12)=\frac{1}{2} S(7)$ and $S(7)>0$, prove that $p_{1}=p_{6}$ and $q_{1}=q_{6}$.
response:
A convenient way to see why one must have p₁ = p₆ and q₁ = q₆ is to write out the condition  
S(2) + S(12) = S(7).  

Since S(i) = ∑(k + ℓ = i) p₍ₖ₎ q₍ℓ₎, we have  
• S(2) = p₁ q₁,  
• S(12) = p₆ q₆,  
• S(7) = p₁ q₆ + p₂ q₅ + p₃ q₄ + p₄ q₃ + p₅ q₂ + p₆ q₁.  

We are given that  
(1) S(2) = p₁ q₁ = ½ S(7),  
(2) S(12) = p₆ q₆ = ½ S(7),  
and S(7) > 0.  

From (1) and (2), it follows that  
p₁ q₁ = p₆ q₆.  
Moreover, adding (1) and (2) shows  
p₁ q₁ + p₆ q₆ = S(7).  

But from the explicit formula for S(7), we see that  
S(7) = p₁ q₆ + p₂ q₅ + p₃ q₄ + p₄ q₃ + p₅ q₂ + p₆ q₁.  
Hence  
p₁ q₁ + p₆ q₆  =  p₁ q₆ + p₂ q₅ + p₃ q₄ + p₄ q₃ + p₅ q₂ + p₆ q₁.  

Move everything to one side to get  
( p₁ q₆ + p₂ q₅ + p₃ q₄ + p₄ q₃ + p₅ q₂ + p₆ q₁ )
− ( p₁ q₁ + p₆ q₆ )  =  0,  
or equivalently,  
p₁ (q₆ − q₁) + p₆ (q₁ − q₆) + (p₂ q₅ + p₃ q₄ + p₄ q₃ + p₅ q₂) = 0.  

Factor out (q₆ − q₁) from the first two terms:

(p₁ − p₆)(q₆ − q₁) + (p₂ q₅ + p₃ q₄ + p₄ q₃ + p₅ q₂) = 0.

Since pᵢ ≥ 0 and qᵢ ≥ 0 for all i, each summand is nonnegative. For their sum to be zero, each summand must itself be zero. Hence:

1) (p₁ − p₆)(q₆ − q₁) = 0,  
2) p₂ q₅ = 0,  
3) p₃ q₄ = 0,  
4) p₄ q₃ = 0,  
5) p₅ q₂ = 0.  

From (1) together with the fact p₁q₁ = p₆q₆ > 0 (which forces p₁, p₆, q₁, q₆ to be nonzero), one sees that the only way to satisfy p₁ q₁ = p₆ q₆ is p₁ = p₆ and q₁ = q₆. Indeed:

• If p₁ = p₆, then p₁ q₁ = p₆ q₆ implies q₁ = q₆ (since p₁ > 0).  
• If q₁ = q₆, then p₁ q₁ = p₆ q₆ implies p₁ = p₆ (since q₁ > 0).

Thus one concludes p₁ = p₆ and q₁ = q₆, as desired.
formatted_prompt: Serge and Lis each have a machine that prints a digit from 1 to 6. Serge's machine prints the digits $1,2,3,4,5,6$ with probability $p_{1}, p_{2}, p_{3}, p_{4}, p_{5}, p_{6}$, respectively. Lis's machine prints the digits $1,2,3,4,5,6$ with probability $q_{1}, q_{2}, q_{3}, q_{4}, q_{5}, q_{6}$, respectively. Each of the machines prints one digit. Let $S(i)$ be the probability that the sum of the two digits printed is $i$. If $S(2)=S(12)=\frac{1}{2} S(7)$ and $S(7)>0$, prove that $p_{1}=p_{6}$ and $q_{1}=q_{6}$.

question:
There are  $200$  numbers on a blackboard: $ 1! ,  2! ,  3! ,  4! , ... ... ,  199! ,  200!$ .
Julia erases one of the numbers. When Julia multiplies the remaining  $199$  numbers, the product is a perfect square. Which number was erased?
formatted_prompt:
There are  $200$  numbers on a blackboard: $ 1! ,  2! ,  3! ,  4! , ... ... ,  199! ,  200!$ .
Julia erases one of the numbers. When Julia multiplies the remaining  $199$  numbers, the product is a perfect square. Which number was erased?
response:
A concise way to see why 100! must be erased is to track everything “mod 2” in the exponents of the prime factorizations.  In other words, for each prime p, we only care whether its total exponent in a product is even or odd.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1) The product of all factorials 1!·2!·3!·…·200!
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Define  
P = 1! × 2! × 3! × … × 200!.

We claim that, when considering exponents modulo 2 for every prime p, the “parity‐vector” of P (i.e., whether each prime’s exponent is even or odd) coincides with the parity‐vector of 100!.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2) Rewriting P in a useful form (mod 2)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Observe that  
k! = 1 × 2 × … × k,  
so  k!  contributes the factors 1,2,…,k  exactly once each.  Hence

P  =  1! × 2! × … × 200!
   =  (1) × (1×2) × (1×2×3) × … × (1×2×…×200).

Instead of trying to multiply them directly, note that each integer m between 1 and 200 appears in exactly those factorials m!, (m+1)!, (m+2)!, …, 200!.  Hence m appears in a total of (200 – m + 1) of the factorials.  Therefore one can rewrite

P  =  ∏(m = 1 to 200)  m^(200 – m + 1).

Now, reduce exponents mod 2.  An exponent (200 – m + 1) mod 2 is the same as (201 – m) mod 2.  Since 201 is odd, we have

 • If m is even, 201 – m is odd ⇒ include m in the product mod 2.  
 • If m is odd, 201 – m is even ⇒ omit m in the product mod 2.

Thus, modulo squares,  
P  behaves like  ∏(m even) m  =  2 × 4 × 6 × … × 200.

But

2 × 4 × 6 × … × 200  =  2^100 × (1 × 2 × 3 × … × 100)  =  2^100 × 100!.

Since 2^100 is itself a perfect square (its exponent is even), that factor “vanishes” mod 2 in prime exponents.  Consequently, the parity‐pattern of P is the same as the parity‐pattern of 100! in the exponents of all primes.  Symbolically,

P ≡ 100!  (mod 2 in every prime exponent).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3) Finding the factorial to remove
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

We want to erase exactly one factorial, say n!, so that the product of the remaining 199 factorials is a perfect square.  Equivalently, removing n! should fix the parity of all prime exponents.  In “vector” language:

 Product of remaining = P / (n!) 

must have all prime exponents even.  Therefore

   (exponent vector of P)  −  (exponent vector of n!)   ≡   0   mod 2.

That is,

   exponent vector of n!   ≡   exponent vector of P   mod 2.

But we just showed that “P mod 2” is precisely the same as “100! mod 2.”  Hence

   n! ≡ 100!  (mod 2 in every prime exponent).

Among factorials, it is 100! itself that has exactly the same prime‐exponent parities as 100!.  (Any smaller factorial has fewer powers of each prime overall; any larger factorial has more—and one cannot pick up exactly an even difference for every single prime without being exactly the same factorial.)

Thus the unique factorial that must be erased is 100!.
